subject: UX & Research
title: The Mere Exposure Effect
description: A 60-second lesson on The Mere Exposure Effect.

hook.text: You hear a song 5 times and hate it. By the 20th play, you're humming along. Familiarity breeds liking.

buildup.text: Zajonc proved it in 1968: people prefer things they've seen before, even without remembering seeing them.

discovery.text: Chinese characters shown 25 times were rated more positively than those shown 5 times — by non-readers.

twist.text: Brand awareness works through mere exposure. You don't need to like a logo — just recognizing it helps.

climax.text: Consistent branding across touchpoints builds mere exposure. Each sighting adds invisible familiarity.

punchline.text: Familiarity breeds preference. Repeated exposure turns strangers into favorites.

quiz.question: How does the mere exposure effect influence brand preference?
options: Repeated exposure to a brand creates unconscious preference even without active engagement
Users must actively evaluate a brand before forming preferences
Mere exposure only works with visual stimuli
